MarketingClaw uses your calendars to show events and scheduling context when you enable calendar access.
MarketingClaw uses your calendars to add events when you enable calendar access.

MarketingClaw uses the camera when you scan a Gateway setup QR code or ask your paired Gateway or assistant to capture a photo or short video from this iPhone, for example to connect to your Gateway or show your assistant a document, device screen, or workspace.
MarketingClaw uses your contacts so you can search and reference people while using the assistant.
MarketingClaw discovers and connects to your MarketingClaw gateway on the local network.
MarketingClaw can share your location in the background when you enable Always.
MarketingClaw uses your location when you allow location sharing.
MarketingClaw uses the microphone for realtime chat, voice wake, push-to-talk, and voice notes.
MarketingClaw may use motion data to support device-aware interactions and automations.
MarketingClaw lets your assistant read photos you allow and lets you choose photos to share.
MarketingClaw uses your reminders to list, add, and complete tasks when you enable reminders access.
MarketingClaw uses on-device speech recognition for talk mode and voice wake.

Used for pnpm/node fallback and PATH population when launching the gateway.
Used by the CLI session loader; stored in ~/.marketingclaw/marketingclaw.json.

SSH keeps the Gateway private; direct is best for HTTPS or Tailscale Serve.

Your personal AI assistant, living on your own Mac.
It answers questions, works with your files and apps, and can chat with you on WhatsApp or Telegram. Setup takes about two minutes.
Give your assistant tasks and let it help across your Mac.
This app, WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, Slack — your choice.
Everything runs where you decide, with permissions you grant.
MarketingClaw can take actions using the permissions and services you enable. Review prompts and only connect tools you trust.
Most people pick this Mac — MarketingClaw installs everything and keeps it running in the background. You can change this anytime in Settings.
Skip Gateway setup for now; pick Local or Remote later in Settings → General.
OK — MarketingClaw won’t start anything yet. Pick Local or Remote later in Settings → General.

The current gateway.remote.token value is not plain text. MarketingClaw for macOS cannot use it directly; enter a plaintext token here to replace it.
These macOS permissions let MarketingClaw automate apps and capture context on this Mac. Status updates automatically.
MarketingClaw is setting up its background service on this Mac. This usually takes under a minute — no Terminal, no administrator password.
Runs quietly and starts again after a restart.
Once the service answers, you’ll connect your AI.
MarketingClaw runs the agent from a dedicated workspace so it can load `AGENTS.md` and write files there without mixing into your other projects.
Create the workspace on the remote host (SSH in first). The macOS app can’t write files on your gateway over SSH yet.
Tip: edit AGENTS.md in this folder to shape the assistant’s behavior. For backup, make the workspace a private git repo so your agent’s “memory” is versioned.
Your agent introduces itself, picks a name with you, and helps you connect WhatsApp, Telegram, or another channel — just chat.
Finish opens the chat — say hi to your new agent.
Pick Local or Remote in Settings → General whenever you’re ready.

On your gateway host: install/update the `marketingclaw` package and make sure credentials exist
(typically `~/.marketingclaw/credentials/oauth.json`). Then connect again if needed.
